channel.title: NYT > Top Stories
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Saudi Arabia and U.A.E. Carried Out Secret Attacks in Iran, U.S. Officials Say
item.description: The Gulf Arab states have been grappling with how to deter Iran after the war made clear the limits of American security guarantees.
item.dc:creator: Eric Schmitt and Vivian Nereim
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Arash Khamooshi for The New York Times
item.media:description: Tehran, the capital of Iran, last week.

item.title: Spies, Sanctions, Cyberattacks: China and the U.S. Clash Behind the Scenes
item.description: After months of avoiding confrontation, the Trump administration has taken recent steps to call out China on Iran, artificial intelligence and spying.
item.dc:creator: David E. Sanger and Dustin Volz
item.media:credit: Kenny Holston/The New York Times
item.media:description: President Trump met with President Xi Jinping of China on Thursday after a flurry of actions from his administration in recent weeks against China.

item.title: Trump’s and Xi’s Body Language at the Summit Mirrored Their Styles
item.description: Although at odds over issues like trade and Taiwan, the U.S. and Chinese leaders met in Beijing with a show of friendly gestures.
item.dc:creator: Alan Yuhas
item.media:credit: Kenny Holston/The New York Times

item.title: Trump Was Flattering, Xi Was Resolute. The Difference Spoke Volumes.
item.description: In contrast to his rhetoric about China at home, President Trump spoke in conciliatory terms with Xi Jinping, the Chinese leader.
item.dc:creator: David E. Sanger
item.media:credit: Kenny Holston/The New York Times
item.media:description: President Trump with President Xi Jinping of China in Beijing on Thursday.

item.title: What People in China Say of Trump
item.description: Residents in four Chinese cities described a mixture of amusement and anger, blaming U.S. tensions for a slowing economy and rising fuel prices.
item.dc:creator: Ana Swanson
item.media:credit: Kenny Holston/The New York Times
item.media:description: President Trump at the state banquet in Beijing on Thursday.

item.title: Mystery Grows Around Representative Thomas Kean Jr.’s Absence
item.description: Representative Thomas Kean Jr. last voted in Washington on March 5, citing a medical issue. An appearance planned for late May has been canceled.
item.dc:creator: Tracey Tully
item.media:credit: Bill Clark/CQ Roll Call, via Associated Press
item.media:description: Thomas Kean Jr., a Republican congressman from New Jersey, has been out of the public eye for more than two months.

item.title: Open Primaries Have Their Fans. The Parties Are Not Among Them.
item.description: New research claims that bipartisan primaries have broad societal benefit, but opposition is growing, and Louisiana voters this weekend will participate in the state’s first closed primary since 1978.
item.dc:creator: Anna Griffin and Kellen Browning
item.media:credit: Tyler Kaufman/Getty Images
item.media:description: Louisiana voters will cast ballots in a closed primary this year for the first time in decades.

item.title: Latino Groups Unite to Win Back Hispanic Voters for Democrats in Midterms
item.description: Four longtime Latino civil rights and political organizations are joining efforts to register voters and mobilize Hispanic Democrats in the wake of rising voting restrictions.
item.dc:creator: Jazmine Ulloa
item.category: League of United Latin American Citizens
item.media:credit: Tamir Kalifa for The New York Times
item.media:description: People waited in line to cast their ballots in the Texas primary election in Austin in March.

item.title: James Talarico’s Taco Order Sparks Debate in Texas Senate Race
item.description: What started as a taco stop with former President Barack Obama quickly turned into a very Texas debate over the proper breakfast taco order.
item.dc:creator: Lauren McGaughy
item.media:credit: Pool photo by Joel Angel Juarez-Pool
item.media:description: Former President Barack Obama ordering tacos alongside Texas Senate candidate James Talarico at the restaurant Taco Joint in Austin earlier this month.

item.title: In a City of Big Dreams, Many Young Adults See a Cloudy Future
item.description: A bleak job market. Rising rents. Huge debt. In New York and other cities, traditional milestones of adulthood feel further away for some 20- and 30-year-olds.
item.dc:creator: Troy Closson
item.media:credit: Tessa Belle Dillman for The New York Times
item.media:description: Soban Ali moved to New York eager to make his way in a new city. Like so many other young adults, he discovered hardship and disappointment.

item.title: N.Y. Takes Two-Step Approach to Tax on Multimillion-Dollar Second Homes
item.description: The tax will initially apply to units with a “market value” of at least $1 million. That metric often underestimates a home’s actual worth.
item.dc:creator: Benjamin Oreskes, Dana Rubinstein and Mihir Zaveri
item.media:credit: Karsten Moran for The New York Times
item.media:description: A proposed tax on high-end second homes in New York City is aimed at raising $500 million a year to help address the city’s budget deficit.

item.title: What Could One Banana Cost? $10? Maybe for You, Some Fear.
item.description: Grocery stores can use shoppers’ personal data to charge different customers different prices, a practice known as surveillance pricing. Lawmakers in New York are considering a ban.
item.dc:creator: Claire Fahy
item.category: Retail, Wholesale and Department Store Union
item.media:credit: Claire Fahy/The New York Times

item.title: Life, Death and Rebirth in Nepal, the Land of the Buddha
item.description: Starting at the birthplace of Buddhism, a writer traces how its teachings spread across Asia, transforming the continent forever.
item.dc:creator: Aatish Taseer and Keerthana Kunnath

item.title: Green Card Holders Targeted for Deportation by New ‘Removal Apparatus’
item.description: The Department of Homeland Security recently formed a unit tasked with revetting thousands of immigrants with permanent residency.
item.dc:creator: Madeleine Ngo
item.media:credit: Wilfredo Lee/Associated Press
item.media:description: About 2,890 cases had been reviewed or were still being assessed by the new unit under U.S. Citizenship and Immigration Services as of May 7.

item.title: Justice Dept. Accuses Yale Medical School of Discriminating Against White and Asian Applicants
item.description: The finding was the second time in eight days that the Trump administration had targeted a major medical school over admissions policies.
item.dc:creator: Michael C. Bender
item.media:credit: Christopher Capozziello for The New York Times
item.media:description: Harmeet K. Dhillon, the assistant attorney general for civil rights, said in a letter that since a Supreme Court ruling barring race-conscious admissions policies, Yale employed a holistic review process “to uncover and then use applicants’ race through direct and indirect means.”

item.title: Justice Dept. Aims to Denaturalize Ex-Marine for Sex Crime
item.description: In recent decades, the federal authorities have generally revoked U.S. citizenship from people accused of wrongdoing on their citizenship applications. A new case focuses on a crime committed later.
item.dc:creator: Ernesto Londoño
item.media:credit: Kenny Holston/The New York Times
item.media:description: A Supreme Court decision in 1967 established that American citizenship procured lawfully cannot be involuntarily revoked.

item.title: Cerebras, A.I. Chip Maker, Rises 89% in Market Debut as Tech IPOs Ramp Up
item.description: Cerebras, a Silicon Valley maker of artificial intelligence chips, began trading on the stock market on Thursday, as SpaceX, OpenAI and Anthropic also take steps to go public.
item.dc:creator: Natallie Rocha
item.media:credit: Christie Hemm Klok for The New York Times
item.media:description: Cerebras presages a series of potential “mega I.P.O.s” from A.I.-related firms including OpenAI, Anthropic and Elon Musk’s SpaceX.

item.title: Why A.I. Safety Controls Are Not Very Effective
item.description: Three years after the debut of ChatGPT, fooling A.I. systems into bad behavior is almost trivial.
item.dc:creator: Cade Metz and Tiffany Hsu
item.media:credit: Jason Henry for The New York Times
item.media:description: A.I. systems from companies like OpenAI and Anthropic have guardrails that often prove to be porous.

item.title: Cuba Says It Has Run Out of Oil
item.description: A long-running energy crisis in the island nation reached a breaking point this week, when government officials said they had run out of reserves.
item.dc:creator: Frances Robles
item.media:credit: Lisette Poole for The New York Times
item.media:description: A gas station in Havana this month. In January, the Trump administration cut off oil shipments from Venezuela, which had been supplying a large amount of Cuba’s oil.

item.title: Will Her Daughter Be Safe at Pali High as It Rebuilds From LA Wildfires?
item.description: It’s a nightmare faced by families all around Los Angeles: After wildfire smoke blanketed homes, schools and offices with toxic chemicals, when is it OK to go back?
item.dc:creator: Emily Baumgaertner Nunn and Jenna Schoenefeld
item.category: Palisades Charter High School (Pacific Palisades, Calif)
item.media:credit: Jenna Schoenefeld for The New York Times
item.media:description: Pearl Abrahams, a senior at Palisades Charter High School in California, attended classes — along with 2,400 other students — in an abandoned department store while her school, burned in the Palisades Fire last year, was rehabilitated.

item.title: Inside Jack Schlossberg’s Chaotic Campaign to Revive Camelot
item.description: Erratic behavior and staff turnover have colored Mr. Schlossberg’s bid for a House seat in New York, raising questions about his readiness for office.
item.dc:creator: Nicholas Fandos
item.media:credit: Vincent Alban/The New York Times
item.media:description: Jack Schlossberg, a Kennedy heir who is running to represent much of Manhattan in Congress, has churned through campaign staff at an unusual pace.

item.title: U.S. Set to Drop Charges Against Indian Billionaire Accused of Fraud
item.description: The decision came after a meeting in which a lawyer for the billionaire, Gautam Adani, made an unusual offer, according to people familiar with the matter.
item.dc:creator: Nicole Hong, Ben Protess, William K. Rashbaum and Devlin Barrett
item.media:credit: M. Scott Brauer for The New York Times
item.media:description: Gautam Adani had been accused by federal prosecutors in Brooklyn of defrauding investors.

item.title: Hantavirus Doesn’t Spread Easily, but Officials May Be Downplaying Risks
item.description: The virus is clearly far less contagious than the coronavirus, scientists agree, but they have found cases where it spread among people without direct contact.
item.dc:creator: Apoorva Mandavilli
item.category: Centers for Disease Control and Prevention
item.media:credit: Chris Mcgrath/Getty Images
item.media:description: Passengers from the MV Hondius evacuated from the cruise ship this week in Tenerife, Spain.

item.title: Can Some Very Tiny Particles Cool the Planet? One Tech Company Says Yes.
item.description: Stardust Solutions says its tiny spheres can reflect the sun’s rays without harming people or the environment. Critics say private companies have no business altering Earth’s atmosphere.
item.dc:creator: Eric Niiler
item.media:credit: Avishag Shaar-Yashuv for The New York Times

item.title: Bumble Is Killing the Swipe Feature. What’s Next for Dating Apps?
item.description: Bumble is losing the feature that reshaped how we look for love. Daters may be ready for something more intentional.
item.dc:creator: Catherine Pearson

item.title: I Don’t Think You Can Even Call This Hypocrisy
item.description: The secrets of one of the architects of the religious right are being revealed. One of the secrets is that they weren’t really secrets.
item.dc:creator: David French

item.title: What Autocrats Have in Common With Abusers
item.description: M. Gessen and Rachel Louise Snyder on the parallels between authoritarianism and domestic violence.
item.dc:creator: M. Gessen, Rachel Louise Snyder and Jillian Weinberger
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
Human Rights and Human Rights Violations
item.media:credit: Illustration by The New York Times; photo by Alexey Nikolsky/Getty

item.title: What Are Your Evening Rituals? We Want to Know.
item.description: Do you meditate? Play Wordle? Wear soft pajamas and sip chamomile tea? We’d like to hear about the little routines that help you wind down.
item.dc:creator: Amanda Schupak
item.media:credit: Getty Images

item.title: Flag With Swastikas and Star of David Flown at N.Y.U., Police Say
item.description: The flag appeared on a university building in the heart of Greenwich Village during a graduation week event, and resembled the purple N.Y.U. banners flying on campus.
item.dc:creator: Claire Fahy
item.media:credit: Hiroko Masuike/The New York Times
item.media:description: A flag with swastikas and a Star of David was taken down from a building on the New York University campus.

item.title: How an Image of Washington at Prayer Became a Touchstone for the Right
item.description: A scene inspired by the winter at Valley Forge has become more prominent in the Trump era, along with claims that the United States was founded as a Christian nation.
item.dc:creator: Jennifer Schuessler

item.title: Chud the Builder, Known for Racist Confrontations, Charged With Attempted Murder
item.description: Dalton Eatherly, who goes by Chud the Builder, was arrested after an altercation that ended in gunfire outside a courthouse near Nashville.
item.dc:creator: Remy Tumin
item.media:credit: George Walker IV/Associated Press
item.media:description: A Sheriff’s deputy investigates a shooting scene outside the Montgomery County Courthouse in Clarksville, Tenn.

item.title: Clarence Carter, Singer of Lust-Filled Soul Hits, Dies at 90
item.description: In songs like “Slip Away” and “Back Door Santa,” he performed with the fervor of a backwoods preacher and the bawdy humor of a juke joint.
item.dc:creator: Bill Friskics-Warren
item.media:credit: Gilles Petard/Redferns, via Getty Images
item.media:description: Clarence Carter in 1975. Blind from youth, he had a deep, declamatory baritone and lecherous, full-throated laugh.
